article_title: New York Finalizes the RAISE Act, Setting the Strictest Frontier AI Transparency Rules in the United States

content_preview.title: New York Finalizes the RAISE Act, Setting the Strictest Frontier AI Transparency Rules in the United States
content_preview.summary: Governor Hochul's March 27 chapter amendment transforms New York's RAISE Act from a vague skeleton into binding law, imposing 72-hour incident reporting and mandatory safety frameworks on frontier AI developers before federal preemption efforts can intervene.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

New York has quietly completed one of the most consequential acts in American AI governance. On March 27, 2026, Governor Kathy Hochul signed Senate Bill 8828, a chapter amendment that transforms the Responsible AI Safety and Education Act — known as the RAISE Act — from its original, legally uncertain form into a binding, enforceable framework targeting the developers of the world's most powerful AI systems. The law takes effect January 1, 2027.

The amendment is not a minor technic...

recommendations: Consider adding trusted domains to config/source_allowlist.txt

editor_notes.content_quality: Strong 1,081-word Analysis on the finalized New York RAISE Act. Well-structured: overview of the chapter amendment, detailed breakdown of requirements (Frontier AI Framework, transparency reports, 72-hour incident reporting, catastrophic risk definition), industry reaction, federal preemption shadow, and compliance outlook. The comparison with California's TFAIA 15-day window adds useful context. The inclusion of the model-deception trigger and its AI safety significance is a good editorial insight.
editor_notes.source_verification: 5 sources read — all law firm and legal news analysis of the finalized law. (1) Wiley Rein (wiley.law) — confirms S8828, 10^26 FLOPS threshold, $500M revenue threshold, 72-hour incident reporting, California 15-day comparison, January 1 2027 effective date, penalties $1M first/$3M subsequent. (2) JD Supra — confirms Frontier AI Framework requirements (risk thresholds/mitigations, third-party assessments, cybersecurity for model weights, internal governance, incident response), annual updates. '30-day material modification disclosure' not explicitly confirmed — JD Supra says 'updated annually (or sooner following a material modification)' without specifying 30 days. (3) IAPP — confirms Chris Lehane (OpenAI Chief Global Affairs Officer) quote verbatim: 'the combination of the Empire State with the Golden State is a big step in the right direction' (article truncates the last four words — not a misquote, just abbreviated). CDT 'starting point, not the finish line' quote confirmed. (4) National Law Review — confirms 'constitutional confrontation remains inevitable' verbatim, penalty revision from $30M (original statute) to $3M for subsequent violations confirmed, NYDFS enforcement confirmed. (5) Davis Wright Tremaine (dwt.com) — confirms signed March 27 2026, S8828, NYDFS rulemaking authority, federal preemption discussion, compliance obligations persist pending legal challenge.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: All major claims verified. One minor unconfirmed detail: '30-day material modification disclosure' window — JD Supra confirms material modification triggers early update cycle but does not specify 30 days; not confirmed by other sources either. This is a minor precision issue, not a blocking error. OpenAI quote is slightly truncated (omits 'in the right direction') — technically accurate, not a misquote.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and technically precise throughout. Industry support (OpenAI, CDT) and the federal preemption conflict are both covered. No editorializing.
editor_notes.originality: Prior Machine Herald article on this topic exists: a February 2026 article on the federal effort to dismantle state AI regulation (linked in the body). The RAISE Act's finalization is a distinct development with new and specific content. No prior Machine Herald article on the RAISE Act itself found.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: Approve. Well-reported legal analysis article on a significant state AI governance development. All major claims verified from five legal analysis sources. The law firm sourcing (Wiley, DWT, JD Supra, NatLawReview) is appropriate for a technical compliance piece.
